title: Poisoning — General Approach
oneLiner: Systematic ABC stabilisation, identify toxin, prevent absorption, enhance elimination, give specific antidote, and arrange psychiatric assessment — all deliberate overdoses need a paracetamol level.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Airway compromise** from reduced GCS (≤8) or seizures — *the finding:* absent gag reflex, apnoea, or aspiration — needs rapid sequence intubation (RSI) before any history.
- **Cardiac dysrhythmia** from sodium-channel blockade (e.g. tricyclic antidepressants) or QTc prolongation — *the finding:* wide QRS (>120ms) with axis shift (TCA) or torsades de pointes — needs IV sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50mL bolus (repeat if QRS still wide) and magnesium for torsades.
- **Hypoglycaemia** from sulphonylureas, insulin, or alcohol — *the finding:* capillary glucose <3.0 mmol/L — give IV 50mL 50% glucose (IM glucagon if no IV access). **Always check glucose immediately** before assuming the altered mental state is due to poisoning.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Altered consciousness (drowsy, agitated, coma)
  - Vomiting, seizures, hypotension, brady/tachycardia
  - Respiratory depression or tachypnoea (metabolic acidosis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - GCS (use AVPU quickly), pupils (pinpoint → opioid; dilated → anticholinergic/sympathomimetic)
  - Skin: dry/flushed (anticholinergic), diaphoretic (cholinergic), cyanosis (methaemoglobinaemia)
  - Odour: bitter almonds (cyanide), garlic (organophosphates, arsenic), pear drops (ketones)
  - Bowel sounds: decreased (anticholinergic, opiate) vs hyperactive (cholinergic, TCA?)
  - Temperature: hyperthermia (serotonin syndrome, NMS, sympathomimetics) vs hypothermia (opioid, alcohol)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Toxidrome categories (most likely → less likely):**
  - Opiate/opioid: pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, low GCS → *give naloxone as diagnostic test*
  - Cholinergic (SLUDGE): salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI upset, emesis, bradycardia, miosis → *responds to atropine*
  - Anticholinergic: hot, dry, red skin; dilated pupils; tachycardia; delirium; urinary retention → *physostigmine (rarely used)*
  - Sympathomimetic: dilated pupils, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, agitation → *benzodiazepines*
  - Serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation → *cyproheptadine or benzodiazepines*
  - Sedative-hypnotic (benzodiazepine, ethanol): low GCS, normal pupils, respiratory depression → *flumazenil is high-risk (seizures) – [check local policy]*
- **Splitter:** **Pupil size + response to light** — pinpoint unresponsive = opioid (or cholinergic if SLUDGE); mid-position sluggish = TCA/sedative; dilated unreactive = anticholinergic/sympathomimetic.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG (look for QRS width, QTc interval, Brugada pattern, dysrhythmia)
  - Capillary blood glucose
  - Temperature (tympanic)
  - Pulse oximetry (consider CO-oximetry if inhalational)
  - 12-lead ECG repeated hourly until stable
- **Bloods (routine panel):**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting, CRP
  - Paracetamol level (essential in **all** deliberate overdoses — even if denied)
  - Salicylate level
  - ABG/VBG with lactate, electrolytes, glucose, ketones
  - Ethanol level, osmolal gap (if toxic alcohol suspected)
  - Creatine kinase (if prolonged immobility, seizures, serotonin syndrome)
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR: if aspiration suspected or reduced GCS (look for bronchial wall thickening, ARDS)
  - CT head: only if focal neurology, trauma, or seizures not explained by overdose (uncommon)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine toxicology screen (point-of-care immunassay) — beware false positives/negatives; rarely changes acute management
  - Specific serum drug levels: digoxin, lithium, theophylline, carbamazepine, valproate (if history or toxidrome consistent)
- **Never-miss test:** **Paracetamol level** in any deliberate overdose (even if patient denies paracetamol) — do not wait for results to start acetylcysteine if high-risk history (e.g. >150 mg/kg, staggered, or unknown time). Also **pregnancy test** in women — modifies management and disposition.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - A → B → C → D → E (secure airway, give O₂, IV access, monitor)
  - Treat life-threatening arrhythmias: wide QRS → sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 mL IV; torsades → magnesium sulfate 2g IV
  - Specific antidotes *after ABC*:
    - Opioid: naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV, repeat every 2-3 min as needed (titrate to respiratory rate, not GCS)
    - Paracetamol: start acetylcysteine if risk (time-dependent, consult TOXBASE or BNF)
    - Benzodiazepine reversal: flumazenil — use only if pure benzodiazepine overdose with respiratory depression AND no seizure risk (e.g. no TCA co-ingestion, no chronic benzo use) — [check local policy]
    - Organophosphate: atropine 2 mg IV every 5-10 min until drying of secretions, then pralidoxime if neuromuscular weakness
  - GI decontamination:
    - Activated charcoal (50 g) within **1 hour** of ingestion, if airway protected (GCS ≥13 without seizure) and no hydrocarbon/caustic/acid poison
    - Gastric lavage only if life-threatening ingestion within 1 hour and airway secured — almost never needed
    - Whole bowel irrigation: sustained-release iron, lithium, heavy metals (rare; consult toxicology)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Supportive care: IV fluids (judiciously), monitor GCS hourly, cardiac monitor (telemetry) for QTc prolongation or dysrhythmia
  - Repeat relevant drug levels (paracetamol, salicylate, etc.) per protocol
  - Psychiatric assessment (liaison psychiatry, crisis team) — **all deliberate overdoses** before discharge
  - Manage withdrawal if chronic alcohol/benzodiazepine use (CIWA-Ar or benzodiazepine protocol)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Referral to drug/alcohol services, mental health follow-up
  - If occupational or accidental exposure: public health notification, workplace safety review
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/h unless specific fluid needed (e.g. sodium bicarb for TCA)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin subcut once daily if not bleeding and not high risk (e.g. overdose on anticoagulants)
  - Diet/NBM: nil-by-mouth if GCS <13 or charcoal given (until reassessed)
  - Observation: NEWS2 chart hourly for first 4-6 hours, then 2-hourly if stable; continuous cardiac monitoring for QTc risk
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Naloxone: 0.4–2 mg IV, repeat q2-3min; dilute in 10 mL saline for slower administration if needed (avoid pulmonary oedema in opioid-dependent)
  - Acetylcysteine (paracetamol): 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% glucose over 60 min, then 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 16 h (or 3-bag BNF regimen). **Always confirm dosing with local protocol.**
  - Flumazenil: reserved for pure benzodiazepine overdose with respiratory depression — 200 mcg IV over 15 sec; if no response after 30 sec, give 100 mcg; max 1 mg. **Use with extreme caution** (risk of seizures, especially with history of epilepsy, TCA co-ingestion, or chronic benzo use).
  - Sodium bicarbonate 8.4%: 50 mL IV over 5–10 min for wide QRS (repeat if QRS still >120 ms after 20 min)
  - Atropine (organophosphate): 2 mg IV push, double dose every 5 min until pulmonary secretions clear
  - Pralidoxime: [check local policy] — bolus 30 mg/kg over 15 min then infusion for organophosphate.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Octreotide (sulphonylurea overdose): 50–100 mcg subcut q6h (if refractory hypoglycaemia)
  - Methanol/ethylene glycol: fomepizole (if available) or ethanol infusion — [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Activated charcoal: do not give after hydrocarbon ingestion (aspiration risk), caustic/acid (perforation), or if airway not protected
  - Flumazenil: avoid if any co-ingestant with pro-convulsant effect (TCA, lithium, isoniazid, cocaine)
  - Acetylcysteine: caution if known asthma (anaphylactoid reactions — slow infusion rate, pre-treat with antihistamines)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Paracetamol overdose:** starting acetylcysteine too late (beyond 8–10 hours) dramatically reduces efficacy **or** using incorrect weight-based dosing in obese patients (use ideal body weight per local policy)
  - **Naloxone:** giving a large bolus (≥0.4 mg) to a known opioid-dependent patient — can precipitate severe opioid withdrawal with vomiting, aspiration, pulmonary oedema, and aggression. Titrate to effect (0.04 mg increments if possible).
  - **Flumazenil:** giving in a mixed overdose (e.g. benzodiazepine + TCA) can cause refractory seizures — never use routinely.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Aspiration pneumonitis (from vomiting or depressed GCS)
  - Acute respiratory failure (APO from opioid, aspiration, ARDS)
  - Seizures (TCA, bupropion, tramadol, alcohol withdrawal)
  - Cardiac arrest (wide QRS, ventricular arrhythmias, asystole from β-blocker/CCB overdose)
  - Rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury (prolonged immobility, serotonin syndrome, neuroleptic malignant syndrome)
  - Hypoglycaemia, electrolyte disturbances
  - Hepatic failure (paracetamol, Amanita phalloides)
- **Chronic:**
  - Encephalopathy (chronic carbon monoxide, lead)
  - Neuropathy (organophosphate, heavy metals)
  - Pulmonary fibrosis (paraquat, chemotherapeutic agents)
  - Psychological sequelae (depression, suicide risk)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Overall mortality in hospitalised overdose** is low (<1%) with timely supportive care and antidotes — **delay in recognising life-threatening toxidrome** is the main modifiable risk factor.
- Paracetamol: excellent prognosis if acetylcysteine given ≤8 hours; acute liver failure risk rises steeply after 10–12 hours (mortality 20–40% when transplant criteria met).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always get collateral history** from paramedics, family, or pharmacy — empty pill bottles, timing, co-ingestants, and previous suicide attempts are often missed.
- **Check TOXBASE** (UK/Ireland) or **NPIS** (phone 0344 892 0111) for every unfamiliar overdose — never guess management for a toxin you have not seen before.
- **Document the “no suicide contract”** and psychiatric plan in discharge summary — this is a medico-legal priority.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT rely on urine drug screen alone** — it has a 15% false-negative rate for many drugs (e.g. synthetic opioids, benzodiazepines) and does not quantify; always combine with serum levels and clinical picture.
- **Do NOT induce vomiting** (ipecac) — it does not improve outcome and risks aspiration or oesophageal injury.
- **Do NOT use flumazenil routinely** — it can cause severe seizures and is almost never needed; if in doubt, call NPIS.
- **Do NOT discharge without psychiatric assessment** — all self-harm overdoses need a mental health evaluation before leaving the ward.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Any new confusion, drowsiness, or difficulty waking
  - Breathing problems (shortness of breath, noisy breathing)
  - Vomiting more than once, especially if blood or coffee-ground
  - New seizure or altered behaviour (aggression, agitation)
  - Jaundice, dark urine, or right upper quadrant pain (paracetamol — may present >24h)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Liaison psychiatry (within 24–72 hours) — ensure crisis plan is in place
  - GP letter detailing toxin, treatment given, and mental health plan
  - If occupational exposure: OH referral and reporting to HSE under RIDDOR
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (SpR/Reg)** if:
  - Any cardiac dysrhythmia or ECG widening >120 ms
  - GCS <13 on arrival or dropping
  - Seizures (any) — ensure IV lorazepam 4 mg or diazepam 10 mg ready
  - NEW S2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4.0 mmol/L
  - Requiring >2 antidote doses (e.g. repeated naloxone, multiple bicarb boluses)
- **Call ICU/Critical Care** if:
  - GCS ≤8 (need airway protection — RSI)
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 despite fluid)
  - Need for vasopressors (noradrenaline), dialysis, or ECMO (e.g. refractory calcium channel blocker overdose)
  - Severe acidosis (pH <7.2) not correcting
  - Any sign of multi-organ dysfunction
- **Call NPIS (0344 892 0111)** if you are unsure of the toxin, need specific antidote advice (e.g. fomepizole, digoxin Fab), or management of rare/envenomation cases. They are 24/7.

rawMarkdown:
# Poisoning — General Approach
**One-liner:** Systematic ABC stabilisation, identify toxin, prevent absorption, enhance elimination, give specific antidote, and arrange psychiatric assessment — all deliberate overdoses need a paracetamol level.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Airway compromise** from reduced GCS (≤8) or seizures — *the finding:* absent gag reflex, apnoea, or aspiration — needs rapid sequence intubation (RSI) before any history.
- **Cardiac dysrhythmia** from sodium-channel blockade (e.g. tricyclic antidepressants) or QTc prolongation — *the finding:* wide QRS (>120ms) with axis shift (TCA) or torsades de pointes — needs IV sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50mL bolus (repeat if QRS still wide) and magnesium for torsades.
- **Hypoglycaemia** from sulphonylureas, insulin, or alcohol — *the finding:* capillary glucose <3.0 mmol/L — give IV 50mL 50% glucose (IM glucagon if no IV access). **Always check glucose immediately** before assuming the altered mental state is due to poisoning.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Altered consciousness (drowsy, agitated, coma)
  - Vomiting, seizures, hypotension, brady/tachycardia
  - Respiratory depression or tachypnoea (metabolic acidosis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - GCS (use AVPU quickly), pupils (pinpoint → opioid; dilated → anticholinergic/sympathomimetic)
  - Skin: dry/flushed (anticholinergic), diaphoretic (cholinergic), cyanosis (methaemoglobinaemia)
  - Odour: bitter almonds (cyanide), garlic (organophosphates, arsenic), pear drops (ketones)
  - Bowel sounds: decreased (anticholinergic, opiate) vs hyperactive (cholinergic, TCA?)
  - Temperature: hyperthermia (serotonin syndrome, NMS, sympathomimetics) vs hypothermia (opioid, alcohol)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Toxidrome categories (most likely → less likely):**
  - Opiate/opioid: pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, low GCS → *give naloxone as diagnostic test*
  - Cholinergic (SLUDGE): salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI upset, emesis, bradycardia, miosis → *responds to atropine*
  - Anticholinergic: hot, dry, red skin; dilated pupils; tachycardia; delirium; urinary retention → *physostigmine (rarely used)*
  - Sympathomimetic: dilated pupils, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, agitation → *benzodiazepines*
  - Serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia, clonus, hyperreflexia, agitation → *cyproheptadine or benzodiazepines*
  - Sedative-hypnotic (benzodiazepine, ethanol): low GCS, normal pupils, respiratory depression → *flumazenil is high-risk (seizures) – [check local policy]*
- **Splitter:** **Pupil size + response to light** — pinpoint unresponsive = opioid (or cholinergic if SLUDGE); mid-position sluggish = TCA/sedative; dilated unreactive = anticholinergic/sympathomimetic.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - ECG (look for QRS width, QTc interval, Brugada pattern, dysrhythmia)
  - Capillary blood glucose
  - Temperature (tympanic)
  - Pulse oximetry (consider CO-oximetry if inhalational)
  - 12-lead ECG repeated hourly until stable
- **Bloods (routine panel):**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, clotting, CRP
  - Paracetamol level (essential in **all** deliberate overdoses — even if denied)
  - Salicylate level
  - ABG/VBG with lactate, electrolytes, glucose, ketones
  - Ethanol level, osmolal gap (if toxic alcohol suspected)
  - Creatine kinase (if prolonged immobility, seizures, serotonin syndrome)
  - Pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR: if aspiration suspected or reduced GCS (look for bronchial wall thickening, ARDS)
  - CT head: only if focal neurology, trauma, or seizures not explained by overdose (uncommon)
- **Special tests:**
  - Urine toxicology screen (point-of-care immunassay) — beware false positives/negatives; rarely changes acute management
  - Specific serum drug levels: digoxin, lithium, theophylline, carbamazepine, valproate (if history or toxidrome consistent)
- **Never-miss test:** **Paracetamol level** in any deliberate overdose (even if patient denies paracetamol) — do not wait for results to start acetylcysteine if high-risk history (e.g. >150 mg/kg, staggered, or unknown time). Also **pregnancy test** in women — modifies management and disposition.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - A → B → C → D → E (secure airway, give O₂, IV access, monitor)
  - Treat life-threatening arrhythmias: wide QRS → sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 mL IV; torsades → magnesium sulfate 2g IV
  - Specific antidotes *after ABC*:
    - Opioid: naloxone 0.4-2 mg IV, repeat every 2-3 min as needed (titrate to respiratory rate, not GCS)
    - Paracetamol: start acetylcysteine if risk (time-dependent, consult TOXBASE or BNF)
    - Benzodiazepine reversal: flumazenil — use only if pure benzodiazepine overdose with respiratory depression AND no seizure risk (e.g. no TCA co-ingestion, no chronic benzo use) — [check local policy]
    - Organophosphate: atropine 2 mg IV every 5-10 min until drying of secretions, then pralidoxime if neuromuscular weakness
  - GI decontamination:
    - Activated charcoal (50 g) within **1 hour** of ingestion, if airway protected (GCS ≥13 without seizure) and no hydrocarbon/caustic/acid poison
    - Gastric lavage only if life-threatening ingestion within 1 hour and airway secured — almost never needed
    - Whole bowel irrigation: sustained-release iron, lithium, heavy metals (rare; consult toxicology)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Supportive care: IV fluids (judiciously), monitor GCS hourly, cardiac monitor (telemetry) for QTc prolongation or dysrhythmia
  - Repeat relevant drug levels (paracetamol, salicylate, etc.) per protocol
  - Psychiatric assessment (liaison psychiatry, crisis team) — **all deliberate overdoses** before discharge
  - Manage withdrawal if chronic alcohol/benzodiazepine use (CIWA-Ar or benzodiazepine protocol)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Referral to drug/alcohol services, mental health follow-up
  - If occupational or accidental exposure: public health notification, workplace safety review
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/h unless specific fluid needed (e.g. sodium bicarb for TCA)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin subcut once daily if not bleeding and not high risk (e.g. overdose on anticoagulants)
  - Diet/NBM: nil-by-mouth if GCS <13 or charcoal given (until reassessed)
  - Observation: NEWS2 chart hourly for first 4-6 hours, then 2-hourly if stable; continuous cardiac monitoring for QTc risk

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Naloxone: 0.4–2 mg IV, repeat q2-3min; dilute in 10 mL saline for slower administration if needed (avoid pulmonary oedema in opioid-dependent)
  - Acetylcysteine (paracetamol): 150 mg/kg in 200 mL 5% glucose over 60 min, then 50 mg/kg in 500 mL over 4 h, then 100 mg/kg in 1000 mL over 16 h (or 3-bag BNF regimen). **Always confirm dosing with local protocol.**
  - Flumazenil: reserved for pure benzodiazepine overdose with respiratory depression — 200 mcg IV over 15 sec; if no response after 30 sec, give 100 mcg; max 1 mg. **Use with extreme caution** (risk of seizures, especially with history of epilepsy, TCA co-ingestion, or chronic benzo use).
  - Sodium bicarbonate 8.4%: 50 mL IV over 5–10 min for wide QRS (repeat if QRS still >120 ms after 20 min)
  - Atropine (organophosphate): 2 mg IV push, double dose every 5 min until pulmonary secretions clear
  - Pralidoxime: [check local policy] — bolus 30 mg/kg over 15 min then infusion for organophosphate.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Octreotide (sulphonylurea overdose): 50–100 mcg subcut q6h (if refractory hypoglycaemia)
  - Methanol/ethylene glycol: fomepizole (if available) or ethanol infusion — [check local policy]
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Activated charcoal: do not give after hydrocarbon ingestion (aspiration risk), caustic/acid (perforation), or if airway not protected
  - Flumazenil: avoid if any co-ingestant with pro-convulsant effect (TCA, lithium, isoniazid, cocaine)
  - Acetylcysteine: caution if known asthma (anaphylactoid reactions — slow infusion rate, pre-treat with antihistamines)
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Paracetamol overdose:** starting acetylcysteine too late (beyond 8–10 hours) dramatically reduces efficacy **or** using incorrect weight-based dosing in obese patients (use ideal body weight per local policy)
  - **Naloxone:** giving a large bolus (≥0.4 mg) to a known opioid-dependent patient — can precipitate severe opioid withdrawal with vomiting, aspiration, pulmonary oedema, and aggression. Titrate to effect (0.04 mg increments if possible).
  - **Flumazenil:** giving in a mixed overdose (e.g. benzodiazepine + TCA) can cause refractory seizures — never use routinely.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Aspiration pneumonitis (from vomiting or depressed GCS)
  - Acute respiratory failure (APO from opioid, aspiration, ARDS)
  - Seizures (TCA, bupropion, tramadol, alcohol withdrawal)
  - Cardiac arrest (wide QRS, ventricular arrhythmias, asystole from β-blocker/CCB overdose)
  - Rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury (prolonged immobility, serotonin syndrome, neuroleptic malignant syndrome)
  - Hypoglycaemia, electrolyte disturbances
  - Hepatic failure (paracetamol, Amanita phalloides)
- **Chronic:**
  - Encephalopathy (chronic carbon monoxide, lead)
  - Neuropathy (organophosphate, heavy metals)
  - Pulmonary fibrosis (paraquat, chemotherapeutic agents)
  - Psychological sequelae (depression, suicide risk)

### Prognosis
- **Overall mortality in hospitalised overdose** is low (<1%) with timely supportive care and antidotes — **delay in recognising life-threatening toxidrome** is the main modifiable risk factor.
- Paracetamol: excellent prognosis if acetylcysteine given ≤8 hours; acute liver failure risk rises steeply after 10–12 hours (mortality 20–40% when transplant criteria met).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always get collateral history** from paramedics, family, or pharmacy — empty pill bottles, timing, co-ingestants, and previous suicide attempts are often missed.
- **Check TOXBASE** (UK/Ireland) or **NPIS** (phone 0344 892 0111) for every unfamiliar overdose — never guess management for a toxin you have not seen before.
- **Document the “no suicide contract”** and psychiatric plan in discharge summary — this is a medico-legal priority.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT rely on urine drug screen alone** — it has a 15% false-negative rate for many drugs (e.g. synthetic opioids, benzodiazepines) and does not quantify; always combine with serum levels and clinical picture.
- **Do NOT induce vomiting** (ipecac) — it does not improve outcome and risks aspiration or oesophageal injury.
- **Do NOT use flumazenil routinely** — it can cause severe seizures and is almost never needed; if in doubt, call NPIS.
- **Do NOT discharge without psychiatric assessment** — all self-harm overdoses need a mental health evaluation before leaving the ward.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Any new confusion, drowsiness, or difficulty waking
  - Breathing problems (shortness of breath, noisy breathing)
  - Vomiting more than once, especially if blood or coffee-ground
  - New seizure or altered behaviour (aggression, agitation)
  - Jaundice, dark urine, or right upper quadrant pain (paracetamol — may present >24h)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Liaison psychiatry (within 24–72 hours) — ensure crisis plan is in place
  - GP letter detailing toxin, treatment given, and mental health plan
  - If occupational exposure: OH referral and reporting to HSE under RIDDOR

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (SpR/Reg)** if:
  - Any cardiac dysrhythmia or ECG widening >120 ms
  - GCS <13 on arrival or dropping
  - Seizures (any) — ensure IV lorazepam 4 mg or diazepam 10 mg ready
  - NEW S2 ≥7
  - Lactate >4.0 mmol/L
  - Requiring >2 antidote doses (e.g. repeated naloxone, multiple bicarb boluses)
- **Call ICU/Critical Care** if:
  - GCS ≤8 (need airway protection — RSI)
  - Haemodynamic instability (SBP <90 despite fluid)
  - Need for vasopressors (noradrenaline), dialysis, or ECMO (e.g. refractory calcium channel blocker overdose)
  - Severe acidosis (pH <7.2) not correcting
  - Any sign of multi-organ dysfunction
- **Call NPIS (0344 892 0111)** if you are unsure of the toxin, need specific antidote advice (e.g. fomepizole, digoxin Fab), or management of rare/envenomation cases. They are 24/7.
